items.name: Pointwise convergence
items.statement:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and suppose that $(f_n)$ is a sequence of real-valued functions defined on $I$. We will say that
(i) the sequence $(f_n)$ \textbf{converges pointwise} on $I$ if the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(x)$ exists for each point $x \in I$ and that
(ii) the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n$ \textbf{converges pointwise} on $I$ if the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n(x)$ converges for each point $x \in I$.

A sequence $(f_n)$ of functions on an interval $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ converges pointwise if and only if the following condition holds:
for each $\epsilon > 0$ and for each $x \in I$, there is an $N \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ such that $|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \epsilon$ for all $n \geq N$.

items.name: Uniform convergence
items.statement:
Let $I$ be an interval and suppose that $f, f_1, f_2, \ldots$ are real-valued functions defined on $I$. (The interval may again be open, closed, or neither, and may be of finite or infinite length.)
(i) We will say that the sequence $(f_n)$ \textbf{converges uniformly} to $f$ on $I$ and write "$\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $I$" if, for each $\epsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ such that $|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \epsilon$ for all $n \geq N$ and all $x \in I$.
(ii) We will say that the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n$ \textbf{converges uniformly} to $f$ on $I$ and write "$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $I$" if $\lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{n} f_k = f$ uniformly on $I$ (as defined in (i)), i.e., if, for each $\epsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ such that $|\sum_{k=1}^{n} f_k(x) - f(x)| < \epsilon$ for all $n \geq N$ and all $x \in I$.

items.name: Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous
items.statement: Suppose that $(f_n)$ is a sequence of continuous functions defined on an interval $I$. If there is a function $f$ on $I$ such that $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $I$ then $f$ is continuous on $I$.

items.name: Interchange of uniform limit and integral
items.statement:
Suppose that $(f_n)$ is a sequence of continuous functions defined on an interval $[a,b]$ and assume that there is a function $f$ on $I$ such that $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $[a,b]$. Then
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \int_a^b f_n(x)\,\mathrm{d}x = \int_a^b f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x.$$

items.name: Interchange of uniform limit and derivative
items.statement:
Suppose that $(f_n)$ is a sequence of functions defined on an interval $[a,b]$ and assume that
(i) $f_n$ is $\mathrm{C}^1$ on $[a,b]$ for each $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$,
(ii) there is a function $g$ defined on $[a,b]$ such that $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n' = g$ uniformly on $[a,b]$, and
(iii) there is a point $c \in [a,b]$ for which the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(c)$ exists.
Then there is a function $f$ on $[a,b]$ such that $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $[a,b]$ and $f' = g$.

Suppose that $(f_n)$ is a sequence of $\mathrm{C}^1$ functions defined on an interval $[a,b]$ and assume that there are two functions $f$ and $g$, also defined on $[a,b]$, such that $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $[a,b]$ and $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n' = g$ uniformly on $[a,b]$. Then $f' = g$.

items.name: Weierstrass M-test
items.statement:
Suppose that $(f_n)$ is a sequence of functions defined on an interval $I$ and assume that there is a sequence $(M_n)$ of numbers such that
(i) $|f_n(x)| \leq M_n$ for all $x \in I$ and all $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ and
(ii) the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n$ converges.
Then the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n$ converges uniformly on $I$.

items.name: Distance and convergence in B(I)
items.statement:
Let $I$ be an interval in $\mathbb{R}$.
(i) The \textbf{distance} between two functions $f$ and $g$ in $\mathrm{B}(I)$ is the number $\|f - g\|$.
(ii) A sequence $(f_n)$ in $\mathrm{B}(I)$ is said to \textbf{converge} to a function $f \in \mathrm{B}(I)$ if, for each $\epsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ such that $\|f_n - f\| < \epsilon$ whenever $n \geq N$.
A sequence $(f_n)$ in $\mathrm{B}(I)$ converges to a function $f \in \mathrm{B}(I)$ (in the sense of Definition 6.15) if and only if $\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n = f$ uniformly on $I$.

items.name: Uniformly Cauchy
items.statement: Suppose that $I$ is an interval in $\mathbb{R}$. A sequence $(f_n)$ of functions in $\mathrm{B}(I)$ is said to be \textbf{uniformly Cauchy} on $I$ if, for each $\epsilon > 0$, there exists an integer $N$ such that $\|f_n - f_m\| < \epsilon$ for all $m, n > N$.

items.name: Uniform Cauchy criterion
items.statement: Suppose that $I$ is an interval in $\mathbb{R}$. A sequence $(f_n)$ of functions in $\mathrm{B}(I)$ converges uniformly if and only if it is uniformly Cauchy.

items.name: Completeness of C(I)
items.statement: If $I$ is a closed and bounded interval in $\mathbb{R}$, then every sequence in $\mathrm{C}(I)$ that is uniformly Cauchy converges uniformly to a function in $\mathrm{C}(I)$.

items.name: Radius of convergence of a power series
items.statement:
Consider the power series
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n$$
and the quantity $R = 1 / \left( \limsup_{n \to \infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \right)$, where $1/0 = \infty$ and $1/\infty = 0$.
(i) If $R = 0$, then the power series converges only for $x = c$.
(ii) If $0 < R < \infty$, then the power series converges absolutely for any $x$ satisfying $|x - c| < R$, diverges for any $x$ satisfying $|x - c| > R$, and converges uniformly on $[c - r, c + r]$ for any $r \in (0, R)$.
(iii) If $R = \infty$, then the power series converges absolutely for any $x$ and uniformly on any interval of finite length.

If $P$ is a polynomial function, then
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \sqrt[n]{|P(n)|} = 1.$$

Let the power series
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n$$
have radius of convergence $R$. Then the following statements hold:
(i) if $P$ is a polynomial, then the power series
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P(n) a_n (x-c)^n$$
has radius of convergence $R$;
(ii) if $Q$ is a polynomial function for which $Q(n) \neq 0$ for every $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$, then the power series
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{a_n}{Q(n)} (x-c)^n$$
has radius of convergence $R$;
(iii) if $b \in \mathbb{R}$, then the power series
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n b^n (x-c)^n$$
has radius of convergence $\frac{R}{|b|}$.

items.name: Continuity of power series
items.statement: A power series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n$ whose radius of convergence $R$ is positive is a continuous function on $(c - R, c + R)$.

For any sequence $(a_n)$ and any $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the three power series
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n, \quad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n(x-c)^{n-1}, \quad \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{a_n}{n+1}(x-c)^{n+1}$$
have the same radius of convergence.

items.name: Term-by-term differentiation and integration of power series
items.statement:
If $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n$ is a power series whose radius of convergence $R$ is positive, then
$$\int_c^x \left( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(t-c)^n \right) \mathrm{d}t = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{a_n}{n+1}(x-c)^{n+1}$$
and
$$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x} \left( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n \right) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n a_n(x-c)^{n-1}$$
for all $x \in (c - R, c + R)$.

items.name: Power series are C-infinity
items.statement:
A power series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n$ whose radius of convergence $R$ is positive is a $\mathrm{C}^{\infty}$-function on the interval $(c - R, c + R)$ and
$$\frac{\mathrm{d}^k}{\mathrm{d}x^k} \left( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n \right) \bigg|_{x=c} = k! \, a_k$$
for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$.

items.name: Uniqueness of power series coefficients
items.statement:
If $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} b_n(x-c)^n$ are two power series with positive radii of convergence $R$ and $S$, respectively, and if there is a number $r$ such that $0 < r \leq \min\{R, S\}$ and
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(x-c)^n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} b_n(x-c)^n$$
for all $x \in (c - r, c + r)$, then $a_n = b_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$.

items.name: Taylor's Theorem
items.statement:
Suppose that $f$ is a function defined on an open interval $I$, that $c$ is a point in $I$, and that $n$ is a positive integer. If $f^{(n+1)}$ exists and is continuous on $I$ and if $R_n$ is as above, then
$$R_n(x) = \frac{1}{n!} \int_c^x (x-u)^n f^{(n+1)}(u)\,\mathrm{d}u$$
and there is a point $\xi \in I$ such that
$$R_n(x) = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x-c)^{n+1}.$$
